title: Cat Polyp Protruding from Ear: Symptoms, Urgency, and Care Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat showing a growth in their ear? Learn about feline inflammatory polyps, urgency levels, and how a photo helps your vet with triage.
content_html:
What is a Feline Inflammatory Polyp?

An inflammatory polyp is a non-cancerous, fleshy growth that typically originates from the lining of the middle ear or the Eustachian tube. In many cases, these growths can enlarge and push through the eardrum, becoming visible as a pink or red mass within the external ear canal. While they are not malignant tumors, they can cause chronic irritation, discharge, and persistent ear infections that do not resolve with standard topical treatments.

Is This a Veterinary Emergency?

The urgency level for a visible ear polyp is categorized as Medium. While a polyp is rarely an immediate life-threatening emergency, it is a source of significant discomfort and can lead to serious complications if the infection spreads to the inner ear. You should aim to have your cat evaluated by a veterinarian within 24 to 48 hours. If you notice your cat has a severe head tilt, is stumbling, or is pawing aggressively at the ear, please contact an urgent care facility sooner.

How a Photo Helps with Triage

Providing a high-quality, well-lit photograph of the growth is incredibly helpful for your veterinary team. Because many ear conditions can look similar—such as tumors, hematomas, or severe inflammation—a photo allows the triage nurse or veterinarian to assess the mass's appearance before you arrive. This helps them determine the level of pain your cat might be in and ensures they have the correct diagnostic equipment, such as an otoscope or sedation protocols, ready for your visit.

faqs.question: Can ear polyps go away on their own?
faqs.answer: No, inflammatory polyps generally do not resolve without veterinary intervention. Most require surgical removal or a procedure called traction-avulsion to prevent them from growing back.

faqs.question: Will my cat need surgery for an ear polyp?
faqs.answer: In many cases, yes. While some polyps can be removed via 'plucking' under sedation, others may require a more involved surgical procedure, such as a ventral bulla osteotomy, to remove the root of the polyp in the middle ear.

faqs.question: Are ear polyps painful for cats?
faqs.answer: Yes, they can be quite painful. Polyps often trap bacteria and debris in the ear canal, leading to pressure, inflammation, and chronic infections that cause your cat significant distress.
